section.title: Peter's Deliverance and the Death of Herod
section.sectionRationale: The gospel has just leapt to the Gentiles at Antioch, and now the church in Jerusalem meets a fresh wave of state persecution. Herod kills James and seizes Peter, but the praying church watches God break iron chains open and, soon after, strike down the proud king. The chapter is a study in two thrones: the earthly king who takes glory to himself and is eaten by worms, and the heavenly King whose word cannot be chained.
section.thesis: A king may kill an apostle and imprison another, but the ascended Christ hears His praying church, rescues His servant, judges the ruler who steals God's glory, and keeps His word spreading and multiplying no matter who opposes it.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: James killed and Peter imprisoned while the church prays; the night rescue by the angel; the joyful confusion at Mary's house; and the death of Herod, closing with the word of God still advancing.

units.label: James killed, Peter imprisoned
units.note: the sword falls, but the church prays

verses.text: About that time, King Herod reached out to harm some who belonged to the church.
He had James, the brother of John, put to death with the sword.
And seeing that this pleased the Jews, Herod proceeded to seize Peter during the Feast of Unleavened Bread.
He arrested him and put him in prison, handing him over to be guarded by four squads of four soldiers each. Herod intended to bring him out to the people after the Passover.
So Peter was kept in prison, but the {{ekklesia|church}} was fervently praying to God for him.

units.label: The angel and the open gate
units.note: chains fall off, iron gates open by themselves

verses.text: On the night before Herod was to bring him to trial, Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains, with sentries standing guard at the entrance to the prison.
Suddenly an {{angelos|angel}} of the Lord appeared and a light shone in the cell. He tapped Peter on the side and woke him up, saying, “Get up quickly.” And the chains fell off his wrists.
“Get dressed and put on your sandals,” said the angel. Peter did so, and the angel told him, “Wrap your cloak around you and follow me.”
So Peter followed him out, but he was unaware that what the angel was doing was real. He thought he was only seeing a vision.

They passed the first and second guards and came to the iron gate leading to the city, which opened for them by itself. When they had gone outside and walked the length of one block, the angel suddenly left him.
Then Peter came to himself and said, “Now I know for sure that the Lord has sent His angel and rescued me from Herod’s grasp and from everything the Jewish people were anticipating.”

units.label: The knock at Mary's gate
units.note: the praying church can hardly believe its own prayer

verses.text: And when he had realized this, he went to the house of Mary the mother of John, also called Mark, where many people had gathered together and were praying.
He knocked at the outer gate, and a servant girl named Rhoda came to answer it.
When she recognized Peter’s voice, she was so overjoyed that she forgot to open the gate, but ran inside and announced, “Peter is standing at the gate!”
“You are out of your mind,” they told her. But when she kept insisting it was so, they said, “It must be his angel.”
But Peter kept on knocking, and when they opened the door and saw him, they were astounded.
Peter motioned with his hand for silence, and he described how the Lord had brought him out of the prison. “Send word to James and to the brothers,” he said, and he left for another place.

units.label: The death of Herod
units.note: the king who took God's glory is struck down

verses.text: At daybreak there was no small commotion among the soldiers as to what had become of Peter.
After Herod had searched for him unsuccessfully, he examined the guards and ordered that they be executed. Then he went down from Judea to Caesarea and spent some time there.

Now Herod was in a furious dispute with the people of Tyre and Sidon, and they convened before him. Having secured the support of Blastus, the king’s chamberlain, they asked for peace, because their region depended on the king’s country for food.
On the appointed day, Herod donned his royal robes, sat on his throne, and addressed the people.
And they began to shout, “This is the voice of a god, not a man!”
Immediately, because Herod did not give glory to God, an angel of the Lord struck him down, and he was eaten by worms and died.

units.label: The word keeps spreading
units.note: the true King's word cannot be chained

verses.text: But the {{logos|word}} of God continued to spread and multiply.
When Barnabas and Saul had fulfilled their mission to Jerusalem, they returned, bringing with them John, also called Mark.

ekklesia.gloss: The assembly, the called-out people of God. Luke sets the praying ekklesia over against the armed power of Herod. The church has no sword and no political leverage, only prayer, yet it is the church, not the king, that prevails.

angelos.gloss: A messenger, an angel. The same word carries two errands in this chapter: an angel of the Lord frees Peter and, in verse 23, an angel of the Lord strikes Herod. The unseen court of heaven serves the ascended Christ, rescuing His servant and judging His rival.

logos.gloss: Word, message. The logos of God is the true subject of Acts. Kings die and apostles are jailed, but the word of God spreads and multiplies, growing like a living thing that no prison and no throne can contain.

basic.context: The church at Antioch has just been born and the gospel is reaching Gentiles. Back in Jerusalem, King Herod Agrippa launches a persecution to win favour with his people. He kills the apostle James and arrests Peter, planning a public execution after Passover. The church cannot fight back, so it does the one thing it can: it gathers and prays through the night. God answers by sending an angel who walks Peter straight out of a maximum-security prison, and not long after strikes down the king who lets the crowd hail him as a god.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter as a deliberate contrast between two rulers. Herod grasps at glory and ends eaten by worms; the ascended Christ quietly rescues His servant and keeps His word advancing. Notice that God did not spare James but did rescue Peter, so the point is not that the faithful never suffer, but that God reigns over life, death, prison, and thrones. The last verse is the punchline: the word of God continued to spread and multiply.
basic.originalLanguages: Three words carry the chapter. The praying ekklesia, the church, is set against the king's soldiers. The angelos, the angel of the Lord, both frees Peter and later strikes Herod, showing heaven's court at work. And the logos, the word of God, is the true hero: it keeps spreading and multiplying while a king lies dead.
basic.typology: Peter's rescue echoes the exodus: it happens at Passover, chains fall, gates open, and God leads His servant out by night, just as He led Israel out of Egypt. The pattern points to the greater rescue Christ works, leading His people out of the prison of sin and death.
basic.christ: Peter walks free, but the pattern points beyond him. Where Peter was delivered from death, Christ went through death and out the other side, His tomb opened not by an angel unlocking a gate but by resurrection power. The ascended Christ is the true King whose word cannot be chained, in contrast to Herod who steals glory and dies.
basic.god: God is sovereign over kings and prisons. He is not absent when James dies, nor overpowered when Peter is chained. He hears the prayers of a powerless church, sends His angel, and will not share His glory with a preening king. He is both the rescuer of His servants and the judge of the proud.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 2 shows the nations raging while God installs His King. Daniel 4 shows a proud king humbled for refusing to honour God. Exodus 12 gives the Passover backdrop of Peter's night rescue. Isaiah 42:8 declares that God will not give His glory to another, the very sin that felled Herod.
basic.oneStory: This chapter belongs to the great movement of Acts: the ascended Christ pours out His Spirit and drives His gospel from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth. Persecution and the death of an apostle cannot stop it, and neither can a king who thinks himself a god. The word of God keeps spreading, and Barnabas and Saul return with Mark, ready for the mission that fills the next chapters.

context.literary: Acts 12 is an interlude between the founding of the Antioch church, chapter 11, and the sending of Barnabas and Saul on mission, chapter 13. It is told with vivid, almost comic detail, the sleepy Peter, the overjoyed Rhoda who forgets to open the gate, and it turns on the sharp contrast between Herod's death and the word's life.
context.historical: King Herod is Herod Agrippa the First, grandson of Herod the Great, who ruled Judea from AD 41 to 44. His courting of Jewish favour and his sudden death at Caesarea are recorded by the historian Josephus as well, who tells of him being hailed as a god and dying in agony days later.
context.canonical: The chapter continues Luke's theme that the word of God grows unstoppably despite opposition, echoing the refrains at Acts 6:7 and 19:20. It sets the stage for the first missionary journey, when the gospel that survives Herod goes out to Cyprus and Asia Minor.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that neither martyrdom nor imprisonment can halt the advance of the gospel, that God hears the prayers of His church, and that He humbles the ruler who claims the glory due to Him alone. Luke frames the whole around the contrast between a dying king and a living word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miraculous rescue describes what God did for Peter on one night; it does not promise that every believer will be freed from prison, as James's death makes plain. What abides is the call to pray, the certainty that God reigns over rulers and outcomes, and the confidence that His word will advance.

debates.question: Why did God rescue Peter but let James be killed?

views.label: Hidden sovereign purpose
views.case: Scripture gives no reason, and the strongest case is to rest in God's wisdom: He appointed Peter for further work and received James as a martyr, and both outcomes served the advance of the gospel. James's death is stated as plainly as Peter's rescue, so Luke will not let us treat deliverance as a formula.

views.label: The church's fervent prayer
views.case: Some point to the church praying earnestly for Peter, verse 5, as the difference. There is real weight here, for Luke deliberately notes the prayer, yet this must not become a rule that enough prayer always secures rescue, since faithful prayer does not always spare the martyr.

debates.bearing: Held rightly, the passage drives us to pray in faith while trusting God's freedom, so that we neither presume upon deliverance nor doubt God's goodness when it does not come.

study.originalLanguages: Luke sets his terms in sharp opposition. The powerless ekklesia, the church, prays while Herod commands soldiers. The angelos, the angel of the Lord, is the hinge of the chapter, freeing Peter in verse 7 and felling Herod in verse 23, one messenger doing the King's twofold work of rescue and judgment. And the closing logos, the word of God that spreads and multiplies, names the true victor, a living word that outlasts the dead king.

typology.fulfillment: The exodus pattern of deliverance at Passover, with chains loosed and gates opened, points to the greater exodus Christ leads, bringing His people out of bondage to sin and death.

typology.type: Herod struck down for stealing glory
typology.fulfillment: The proud ruler who claims divine honour and is judged anticipates the fall of every God-opposing power, and stands opposite the true King who receives worship rightly, Jesus the risen Lord.

typology.type: The word that spreads and multiplies
typology.fulfillment: The living, growing word of God, unstoppable by prison or throne, is the seed of the kingdom that will fill the earth, fulfilled as the gospel reaches the nations.

study.christ: Peter is delivered from death, but he is not the deliverer; his rescue points to Christ, who was not merely brought out of prison but raised out of the grave. Herod plays the false king, grasping glory and dying under judgment, and by contrast Jesus is the true King, the one whose word cannot be chained and whose throne does not fall. The Lord who sent His angel to free Peter is the ascended Christ ruling over kings and courts, driving His gospel to the ends of the earth.
study.god: God reigns over the whole scene. He permits James to die and chooses to rescue Peter, and He is just and good in both. He bends His ear to a praying church that has nothing but prayer, and He acts with quiet power against overwhelming force. And He guards His own glory: the king who accepts worship as a god is struck down at once, for God will not give His glory to another.

crossReferences.note: The nations and their kings rage against the Lord and His Anointed, yet God installs His King; Herod rages and falls while Christ reigns.
A proud king is humbled for failing to honour God, the pattern behind Herod's sudden judgment.
The Passover setting of Peter's night deliverance, chains loosed and the servant led out, echoes the exodus rescue.
The Lord will not give His glory to another; Herod dies for taking to himself the honour due to God alone.

study.oneStory: Acts traces how the ascended Christ pours out His Spirit and pushes the gospel outward from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth. Here a hostile king tries to strangle the movement by the sword and the prison, and fails on both counts: Peter walks free, Herod is struck down, and the word of God keeps spreading and multiplying. The chapter ends with Barnabas and Saul returning with Mark, poised for the first missionary journey through which the unstoppable word will reach the nations.

quiz.q: What did the church do while Peter was in prison?

options.text: It prayed fervently to God for him.
options.note: Verse 5 says the church was earnestly praying to God for Peter, the pivot of the whole rescue.

options.text: It gathered anxiously at Mary's house, uncertain whether God would act.
options.note: True to the scene, they did gather and were slow to believe, but Luke's stress falls on their fervent prayer, not their doubt.

options.text: It organized a plan to break Peter out by force.
options.note: There is no armed rescue here; the church's only weapon was prayer, and God sent the angel.

options.text: It kept quiet and did nothing, fearing Herod.
options.note: Far from doing nothing, the church prayed fervently through the night.

quiz.q: How did Peter get out of the prison?

options.text: An angel of the Lord woke him, loosed his chains, and led him past the guards and the iron gate.
options.note: Verses 7 to 10 describe the angel freeing Peter as gates opened by themselves.

options.text: God delivered him through supernatural means while he slept between the soldiers.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it leaves out the specific agent, the angel of the Lord who led him out.

options.text: The guards fell asleep and Peter slipped away on his own.
options.note: The guards were still in place; the deliverance was the angel's doing, not Peter's stealth.

options.text: Herod relented and released him.
options.note: Herod intended to execute Peter and later ordered the guards punished; he did not release him.

options.text: Because he accepted the crowd's praise as a god and did not give glory to God.
options.note: Verse 23 states he was struck because he did not give God the glory.

options.text: Because he had killed James and imprisoned Peter.
options.note: His persecution was real wickedness, but Luke names the immediate cause as his stealing God's glory.

options.text: Because he mismanaged the dispute with Tyre and Sidon.
options.note: That dispute sets the scene, but it was not the reason for the judgment.

options.text: Because he was old and simply fell ill.
options.note: Luke attributes his death directly to God's judgment, not natural causes alone.

quiz.q: What is the point of the chapter's final verse?

options.text: The word of God kept spreading and multiplying despite the king's opposition.
options.note: Verse 24 crowns the chapter: the true King's word is unstoppable.

options.text: Barnabas and Saul returned to Antioch with John Mark.
options.note: That happens in verse 25 and sets up the mission, but the theological climax is the advancing word in verse 24.

options.text: The church finally felt safe from persecution.
options.note: The chapter does not promise safety; it promises the word's advance amid ongoing danger.

options.text: The gospel had reached its limit in Jerusalem.
options.note: The opposite is true: the word keeps multiplying and is about to go to the nations.

quiz.q: How does this chapter contrast the two kings?

options.text: Herod grasps glory and is judged, while the ascended Christ rescues His servant and reigns over all.
options.note: The whole chapter sets the dying, glory-grasping king against the living Lord whose word cannot be chained.

options.text: Herod is powerful on earth, but God is powerful in heaven.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it misses the sharp reversal by which the earthly king falls and the heavenly King prevails.

options.text: Both kings are shown to rule justly in their own spheres.
options.note: Herod does not rule justly; he persecutes the church and steals God's glory.

options.text: The chapter treats Herod as the rightful ruler God endorses.
options.note: God strikes Herod down; he is the false king set against the true.

questions: The church could not fight Herod, so it prayed. When you feel powerless against forces bigger than you, does prayer feel like your first resort or your last? What would change if it were your first?
God rescued Peter but did not spare James. How do you hold on to God's goodness when He delivers one person and not another?
The praying believers could hardly believe Peter was really at the door. Where have you prayed for something and then been surprised, even doubtful, when God answered?
Herod fell because he took glory that belonged to God. Where are you tempted to take credit or applause that you should be handing back to God?
The word of God kept spreading no matter what Herod did. How does the unstoppable advance of the gospel steady you when the church seems weak or under pressure today?

translationNotes.note: King Herod here is Herod Agrippa the First; the BSB footnote makes this explicit. The phrase rendered reached out to harm is literally put forth the hands to mistreat.
The Feast of Unleavened Bread and the Passover, mentioned in verses 3 and 4, set Peter's rescue against the backdrop of Israel's exodus deliverance, a link Luke's readers would feel.

Manuscripts differ over whether Barnabas and Saul returned to Jerusalem or from Jerusalem; the BSB reads returned to Jerusalem, with the alternate from Jerusalem noted. The sense of the mission being fulfilled is the same.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 12. Footnote markers, line breaks, and note objects were skipped when building verse text. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
